content.problemStatement:
Given a sequence of NNN elements, Chef defines a function F(i)F(i)F(i) (1≤i≤N)(1 \leq i \leq N)(1≤i≤N) as the median of the first iii elements of the sequence.

Chef wants to get a permutation PPP of length NNN such that Pi=F(i)P_i = F(i)Pi​=F(i) ∀\forall∀ 1≤i≤N1 \leq i \leq N1≤i≤N. Can you help Chef in achieving this?

Note that:


The median of a sequence is the middle element in the sorted order of the sequence. If NNN is even, choose the element at N2\frac{N}{2}2N​ position in the sorted order. If NNN is odd, choose the middle element.
A permutation of length NNN is an array of NNN integers P=[P1,P2,…,PN]P = [P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_N]P=[P1​,P2​,…,PN​] such that every integer from 111 to NNN (inclusive) appears in it exactly once. For example, [2,5,4,1,3][2,5,4,1,3][2,5,4,1,3] is a permutation of length 555 while [2,5,2,1,3][2, 5, 2, 1, 3][2,5,2,1,3] is not.

Input Format

First line will contain TTT, number of test cases. Then the test cases follows.
Each test case consists of a single line of input, an integer NNN, the length of the required permutation.


Output Format
For each test case, output in a new line, NNN space-separated integers, denoting a permutation satisfying the condition.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 200000
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single integer N, the length of the required permutation.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a line with N space‑separated integers – a permutation P of {1,…,N} such that for every i (1≤i≤N) the i‑th element Pi equals the median of the first i elements of P.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For N=1 the only permutation [1] works. For N=2 the construction gives [2,1]; median([2])=2 and median([2,1])=1. For N=3 we obtain [3,1,2]; medians are 3,1,2 respectively.
Applying the alternating high‑low construction: for N=4 → 4,1,3,2; for N=5 → 5,1,4,2,3. Each prefix’s median equals the last element of that prefix.
Sequence 6,1,5,2,4,3 satisfies the required median property for all prefixes up to length 6.

solution.approach: Observe that for a prefix of length i the median is the element that has exactly floor((i‑1)/2) smaller elements among the previous i‑1 positions. By always placing the current largest unused number, then the current smallest unused number, we guarantee this property: after placing the largest (N), the smallest (1) becomes the median of length 2; then the next largest (N‑1) has exactly one smaller element (1) among the previous two, making it the median of length 3, and so on. The construction is: output N,1,N‑1,2,N‑2,3,… until all numbers are used. This runs in O(N) per test case and uses O(1) extra memory.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, total O(ΣN) ≤ 2·10^5

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it))
        low, high = 1, N
        parts = []
        while low < high:
            parts.append(str(high))
            parts.append(str(low))
            high -= 1
            low += 1
        if low == high:
            parts.append(str(low))
        out_lines.append(' '.join(parts))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
